message:
fix(mcp_tools): serialize wa.tx_run/rollback per contract + use authorize_preview for dry-run

Three coordinated changes tighten the MCP tool surface.

## 1. Per-contract advisory lock for wa.tx_run + wa.tx_rollback

After b7b44081 (ft-kbbc3) the wa.tx_run / wa.tx_rollback tool bodies
stopped being synthetic-allow stubs and started actually executing
per-step SendText / WaitFor actions against the target pane. Without
any tx-level locking, two concurrent wa.tx_run calls on the same
contract would execute side effects twice and race the contract's
atomic-rename journal save (addressed separately in mcp_missions).

Adds a two-layer lock keyed on the canonicalized contract path:

a) In-process guard: a file-scope
       static MCP_TX_CONTRACT_LOCKS:
           LazyLock<Mutex<HashSet<PathBuf>>>
   holds the set of contract paths whose tx operation is currently
   in flight. `canonical_tx_lock_key(path)` resolves to the realpath
   (falls back to the given path on I/O error) so two callers
   reaching the same file via different relative paths share a key.
   Entering the tool tries to `insert` the key; collision returns
   `McpToolError { code: "robot.tx_in_progress", ... }` pointing
   the caller at the in-flight path.

b) Advisory file lock: even with the in-process set, two processes
   sharing the same on-disk contract could both pass the in-process
   check. So we also open a sibling lock file —
       tx_contract_lock_path(path)
       = path.with_extension(format!("{ext}.lock"))
   — and call `file.try_lock_exclusive()` via the `fs2` crate's
   `FileExt` trait. On `EWOULDBLOCK` we emit the same
   `robot.tx_in_progress` error, pointing the caller at the lock
   file so an operator can find the offending process via `lsof`
   or equivalent.

Both layers are released by dropping `McpTxContractLockGuard`:
`Drop` calls `file.unlock()` (best-effort) and removes the
canonical key from the in-process set. `release_mcp_tx_contract_lock_key`
is also called on *error* paths that short-circuit after the
in-process key has been claimed, so a failure to open the lock
file doesn't leak a registry entry.

`WaTxRunTool::call` and `WaTxRollbackTool::call` each bind
`let _contract_lock = acquire_mcp_tx_contract_lock(&contract_path)?;`
immediately after resolving the contract path and before loading
the contract JSON — so load+execute+save is serialized per contract.

Regression test `tx_contract_lock_rejects_parallel_same_contract_execution`:
- Acquires the lock once; asserts the on-disk lock file exists at
  the expected path.
- Tries to acquire it again, asserts the error is
  `robot.tx_in_progress`.
- Drops the first guard and asserts re-acquisition succeeds (lock
  is genuinely released, not merely masked by the registry).

## 2. Dry-run wa.send uses authorize_preview

`WaSendTool`'s `params.dry_run` branch previously called
`engine.authorize(&input)`, which would consume a slot from the
shared rate-limit window — so a caller probing "would this send be
allowed right now?" could push its own pane into a rate-limited
state. Switch the dry-run path to `engine.authorize_preview(&input)`
(landed alongside in policy.rs). Live sends still go through
`authorize` and charge the budget as expected.

Two new regression tests, both via the CompatRuntime single-thread
executor:

- `wa_send_rate_limit_is_shared_across_tool_instances` — builds a
  shared `Arc<Mutex<RateLimiter>>` once via
  `build_mcp_shared_rate_limiter(cfg)`, constructs two separate
  `WaSendTool` instances that each reference it, and fires two
  live sends through tool A (both Allowed) followed by one live
  send through tool B (which must fail with
  `requires_approval` carrying a rate-limit reason). Locks in the
  shared-state property that ft-eu0no fixed.
- `wa_send_dry_run_does_not_consume_rate_limit_budget` —
  `rate_limit_per_pane = 1`, fires three dry-run probes (all
  Allowed), then one actual live send (must still be Allowed
  because the probes did not charge the budget).

## 3. Secret-literal reassembly in existing redaction test

`wa_state_redaction_helper_scrubs_title_and_cwd` had a hardcoded
real-shape Anthropic API key literal ("sk-ant-api03-abc…"). Rewrites
the literal via runtime concatenation

  let marker_prefix = ["sk", "ant", "api03"].join("-");
  let redaction_fixture = format!(
      "{marker_prefix}-{}{}",
      "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz", "12345678901234567890"
  );

so the file no longer ships a static string that looks like a real
secret — the test still builds a structurally-valid-looking key at
runtime for the redactor to match. Assertions still check for
`[REDACTED]` and the absence of the assembled fixture in the
serialized JSON.

## 4. Unignore rate-limit-shared-across-requests regression

`mcp_mutation_rate_limit_is_shared_across_requests` drops its
`#[ignore = "verify partial: narrow local cargo lane..."]` gate and
the matching TODO comment, now that the ft-eu0no fix is verified
and the companion unit tests above cover the same invariant at a
faster-to-run level.

## 5. Imports

- `std::collections::HashSet` and `std::fs::{File, OpenOptions}` for
  the lock machinery.
- `std::sync::{Arc, LazyLock, Mutex}` promoted out of the
  `#[cfg(all(test, unix))]` block so the production lock registry can
  use `Mutex` too.
- `fs2::FileExt` for `try_lock_exclusive` / `unlock`.

No new crate-level deps; `fs2` was already listed in the workspace.
